food_subgroup: Slow-braised beef in coconut milk curry with potatoes, peanuts, and warm spices

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Massaman Curry. Closest parent is a coconut milk-based Southeast Asian beef curry. No exact FoodOn URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Slow-braised beef in coconut milk Massaman curry with potatoes, peanuts, and warm spices (cinnamon, cardamom, cloves)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0GPF used as closest parent for coconut milk-based meat curry; no exact Massaman Curry code exists in FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Massaman Curry as a specific food entity.

serving.description: 1 bowl curry with potato, no rice
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Massaman Curry Beef is approximately 320g, comprising braised beef, potatoes, peanuts, and coconut milk sauce, served without rice.

nutrient_source.title: Massaman Curry Beef — FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Table for coconut milk-based beef curry, adapted for Massaman Curry preparation with potatoes and peanuts. Values cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for beef chuck (braised), coconut milk (canned), and roasted peanuts to validate macro and micronutrient plausibility. The composite dish values reflect a weighted recipe calculation consistent with standard Massaman Curry formulation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Dishes and Curry-Based Foods — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Massaman Curry has no direct GI entry in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 48 (low) is estimated based on analogous coconut milk-based beef curry with potato entries in the database (GI range 45–55). The high fat content from coconut milk (9.5g saturated fat per 100g) and protein from beef substantially attenuate the glycemic response of the potato component. Glycemic load per 320g serving calculated as: (48 × 27.2g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 13.1. This is consistent with the University of Sydney methodology for mixed dish GL calculation.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from fish sauce and shrimp paste
hypertension.guidance: A standard 320g serving delivers approximately 1,136mg sodium (nearly 50% of the 2,300mg daily limit), primarily from fish sauce and shrimp paste in the curry paste; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced fish sauce preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load attenuated by coconut fat and beef protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: The glycemic load of 13.1 per 320g serving is moderate; the high fat and protein content from coconut milk and beef substantially suppress the glycemic response of the potato, making this curry more glycemically favourable than potato-based dishes alone, but portion control remains important for blood glucose management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat from full-fat coconut milk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: At 9.5g saturated fat per 100g (30.4g per 320g serving), this dish significantly exceeds the recommended daily saturated fat limit of 20g for adults; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish occasionally and in small portions, noting that the anti-inflammatory spices (cinnamon, cloves, cardamom) and peanut-derived unsaturated fats provide partial mitigation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spice profile versus high saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Massaman Curry contains clinically meaningful anti-inflammatory spices — cinnamon (cinnamaldehyde), cloves (eugenol, a documented COX-2 inhibitor), and cardamom (cineol) — alongside peanut-derived magnesium and vitamin E; however, the high saturated fat from coconut milk may promote systemic inflammation, so moderate consumption is advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Massaman Curry Beef (แกงมัสมั่นเนื้อ, Gaeng Massaman Nuea) is the most internationally acclaimed Thai curry, rated as one of the world's most delicious dishes by CNN Travel. It is the mildest and richest of Thailand's coconut milk curries, with a flavour profile shaped by Persian and Indian spice trade routes through Southern Thailand's Malay Muslim communities. Unlike the sharp herbal heat of green or red curry, Massaman is slow-braised, deeply aromatic, and subtly sweet-sour, featuring warm dried spices — cinnamon, cardamom, cloves, and star anise — alongside classic Thai curry aromatics. Beef chuck or brisket is slow-braised in full-fat coconut milk with cubed potatoes, roasted peanuts, onion, and bay leaves until the meat is tender and the sauce is thick, mahogany-red, and deeply flavoured. The potatoes are unusual for a Thai curry and contribute meaningful carbohydrate, partially replacing the need for rice. Nutritionally, this is the most calorie-dense of the Thai curries at 168 kcal per 100g, driven by fat from both coconut milk (9.5g saturated fat per 100g) and peanuts. It is also nutritionally complete: protein from beef (10.2g/100g), starch from potato, fibre from peanuts (1.5g/100g), and a broad micronutrient profile including iron (2.2mg), zinc (2.8mg), vitamin B12 (1.2mcg), and potassium (265mg). Sodium is elevated at 355mg/100g from fish sauce and shrimp paste. The glycemic index is estimated at 48 (low) due to fat and protein attenuating the potato GI. This dish is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free, making it suitable for Muslim diners and those with lactose intolerance. It is a staple at Thai restaurants in Singapore and across Southeast Asia.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Table adapted for Massaman Curry preparation, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for key ingredients. No single approved source contains an exact Massaman Curry Beef entry; values represent a defensible recipe-weighted composite. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database range for coconut milk beef curry with potato. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the absence of an exact dish entry in any single approved primary source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for composite Massaman Curry Beef dish not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA FoodData Central as a single dish entry; individual ingredient fatty acid data exists but composite dish-level values are not published in approved sources. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite curry dish is not reported in FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA FoodData Central at the dish level. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): beef and coconut milk in this preparation contain negligible industrially produced trans fats; naturally occurring ruminant trans fats are below detection threshold per USDA SR Legacy data for braised beef and coconut milk. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database range for coconut milk-based beef curry with potato (GI 45–55); no direct Massaman Curry GI measurement exists in the database. Sodium level of 355mg/100g (1,136mg per 320g serving) is clinically significant for hypertension management. Saturated fat of 9.5g/100g (30.4g per 320g serving) is clinically significant for cardiovascular disease management. This dish is suitable for halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free dietary requirements.
